items.title: Genomic Characteristics and Pathogenicity of Shigella
items.content: Shigella is distinguished from E. coli by specific genomic features that contribute to its pathogenicity, including a virulence plasmid and the presence of genes for Shiga toxin in S. dysenteriae type 1.
keyPoints: Genome sequencing shows ~93% gene similarity between E. coli K12 and various Shigella species.

Three major genomic 'signatures' of Shigella: (1) a 215-kb virulence plasmid, (2) lack of genetic sequences for lysine decarboxylase, and (3) presence of genes encoding Shiga toxin in S. dysenteriae type 1.

items.title: Differentiation of Shigella Serovars
items.content: Shigella species can be differentiated from each other and E. coli based on biochemical, serologic characteristics, and specific genomic features.
keyPoints: Shigella is non-spore-forming, gram-negative, nonmotile, does not produce gas from sugars, decarboxylate lysine, or hydrolyze arginine.
Differentiation of Shigella species: S. dysenteriae (serogroup A), S. flexneri (B), S. boydii (C), and S. sonnei (D).
Some serovars produce indole; occasional strains utilize sodium acetate.

items.title: High Infectivity and Transmission Modes of Shigella
items.content: Shigellosis is highly infectious, with a small inoculum (100 CFU) needed for transmission. It spreads primarily via the fecal-oral route, often through hand carriage, but can also be foodborne or waterborne. Outbreaks are common in crowded settings with poor hygiene.
keyPoints: Very small inoculum required for infection: 100 colony-forming units (CFU)
High attack rates during outbreaks: 33–73% in day-care centers
Secondary cases among family members of sick children: 26–33%
Transmission modes: fecal-oral, foodborne, waterborne, and sexual

items.title: Shigellosis Prevalence in Developing vs. Industrialized Countries
items.content: Shigellosis is endemic in developing countries, with 99% of cases occurring there, especially in impoverished areas. S. flexneri is common in the least developed regions, while S. sonnei is more prevalent in economically emerging and industrialized nations.
keyPoints: 99% of shigellosis cases occur in developing countries
S. flexneri predominates in least developed areas
S. sonnei more common in economically emerging and industrialized regions

items.title: Impact of Shigellosis on Child Mortality and Nutrition
items.content: Shigella is a major cause of diarrheal diseases, particularly affecting children under 5. It contributes significantly to mortality and malnutrition in endemic areas, leading to stunted growth.
keyPoints: Top pathogen for moderate to severe diarrhea in children <5 years
8.5-fold increase in mortality incidence over average diarrheal disease-related mortality
Contributes to short- and long-term nutritional impairment and stunted growth

items.title: Mechanism of Shigella Invasion and Spread
items.content: Shigella invades intestinal epithelial cells using a type III secretion system encoded by a large virulence plasmid. This system allows bacterial effectors to enter host cell cytoplasm, inducing uptake into epithelial cells and promoting intracellular movement and cell-to-cell spread.
keyPoints: Virulence plasmid of 214 kb with ~100 genes
25 genes encode a type III secretion system
Induces apoptosis of subepithelial resident macrophages
Bacteria invade epithelial cells by inducing uptake at colonic crypts or via M cells
Effectors trigger cytoskeletal rearrangements for bacterial uptake into epithelial cells
Shigella-containing vacuole is lysed, releasing bacteria into the cytosol
Intracellular shigellae use cytoskeletal components to move and spread cell-to-cell

items.title: Role of Shiga Toxin in Disease Severity and Complications
items.content: Shiga toxin produced by S. dysenteriae type 1 exacerbates disease severity by inhibiting protein synthesis, leading to hemolytic-uremic syndrome (HUS) through its effects on the kidneys.
keyPoints: Shiga toxin is an A1-B5 protein toxin
B subunit binds to globotriaosylceramide receptor
A subunit inhibits protein synthesis by cleaving 28S ribosomal RNA
Inhibits binding of amino-acyl-tRNA to the 60S ribosomal subunit
Translocated into circulation and binds to kidney target cells
Can lead to hemolytic-uremic syndrome (HUS)

items.title: Understanding Shigellosis Phases and Severity
items.content: Shigellosis progresses through four phases: incubation, watery diarrhea, dysentery, and postinfectious. The severity is influenced by the host's age, immunologic status, and nutritional condition, with children under 5 being particularly vulnerable.
keyPoints: Incubation period lasts 1–4 days, up to 8 days.
Initial symptoms include fever (up to $40^{	ext{o}}$C – $41^{	ext{o}}$C), malaise, and anorexia.
Dysentery phase characterized by bloody mucopurulent stools and abdominal cramps.
Severe complications more common in children <5 years and the elderly.

items.title: Identifying Risk Factors for Severe Shigellosis
items.content: Risk factors for severe shigellosis include nonbloody diarrhea, moderate to severe dehydration, bacteremia, absence of fever, abdominal tenderness, and rectal prolapse. Malnourished children are at higher risk.
keyPoints: Major complications: toxic megacolon, intestinal perforations, rectal prolapse, hypoglycemia, hyponatremia.
Bacteremia rare but more frequent in malnourished and HIV-infected patients.
Altered consciousness linked to fever and metabolic alterations.

items.title: Recognizing Complications: Toxic Megacolon and HUS
items.content: Toxic megacolon results from severe colonic inflammation, presenting with abdominal distention. Hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS) is linked to Shiga toxin, characterized by microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and acute renal failure.
keyPoints: Toxic megacolon: abdominal x-ray shows colonic dilation and thumbprinting.
HUS diagnostic triad: hemoglobin <80 g/L, platelets <60,000/μL, elevated creatinine levels.
Acute renal failure occurs in 55–70% of HUS cases.

items.title: Reactive Arthritis Post-Shigellosis
items.content: Reactive arthritis can develop weeks or months after shigellosis, particularly in patients with the HLA-B27 antigen. This condition is associated with S. flexneri infection and manifests as arthritis, ocular inflammation, and urethritis.
keyPoints: 3% of S. flexneri-infected patients may develop reactive arthritis.
Reactive arthritis can last months or years and progress to chronic arthritis.
Occurs only after S. flexneri infection.

items.title: Differential Diagnosis of Dysenteric Syndrome
items.content: In developing areas, consider other pathogens like Salmonella and Campylobacter. In industrialized countries, inflammatory bowel diseases such as Crohn's disease should be considered.
keyPoints: Consider infectious diarrhea from various bacteria or parasites in developing regions.
Distinguish shigellosis from inflammatory bowel diseases through anamnesis.

items.title: Laboratory Diagnosis of Shigella Infection
items.content: The gold standard for diagnosing Shigella is isolating the pathogen from fecal material. Use multiple media types to increase isolation success, and identify colonies by their characteristics.
keyPoints: Isolation of Shigella from feces is essential for diagnosis.
Use buffered glycerol saline or Cary-Blair medium for transport.
Blood cultures positive in fewer than 5% of cases.
Shigellae appear as non-lactose-fermenting colonies on selective media.

items.subtopic: ANTIBIOTIC SUSCEPTIBILITY OF SHIGELLA | ANTIBIOTIC TREATMENT OF SHIGELLOSIS (TABLE 172-1)
items.title: Understanding Shigella Antibiotic Resistance Patterns
items.content: Shigellosis treatment is complicated by increasing antibiotic resistance, which varies geographically. Initially effective drugs like amoxicillin have become less viable due to resistance, leading to the adoption of quinolones such as ciprofloxacin and norfloxacin. However, resistance to these has also emerged, necessitating later-generation quinolones in many regions.
keyPoints: Resistance rates are highly dependent on geographic area.
High rates of resistance to classic first-line antibiotics like amoxicillin have led to the use of quinolones.
Second-generation quinolones (e.g., norfloxacin, ciprofloxacin) were initially effective but faced resistance in 5% of isolates in India and Bangladesh.
In the United States, fluoroquinolone resistance reached 87% during 2014–2015.
items.reference: Shigellosis · ANTIBIOTIC SUSCEPTIBILITY OF SHIGELLA | ANTIBIOTIC TREATMENT OF SHIGELLOSIS (TABLE 172-1)

items.subtopic: ANTIBIOTIC SUSCEPTIBILITY OF SHIGELLA | ANTIBIOTIC TREATMENT OF SHIGELLOSIS (TABLE 172-1)
items.title: Guidelines for Antibiotic Treatment of Shigellosis
items.content: Effective antibiotic therapy is crucial in managing shigellosis, reducing complications and transmission. Fluoroquinolones like ciprofloxacin are first-line treatments, while cephalosporins and β-lactams serve as second-line options. Treatment duration varies based on the Shigella species and patient immune status.
keyPoints: Clinical improvement with antibiotics occurs within 48 hours.
Fluoroquinolones (e.g., ciprofloxacin) are first-line treatments, recommended for 7–10 days.
Non-dysenteriae Shigella infections in immunocompetent individuals typically require a 3-day course.
S. dysenteriae type 1 and infections in immunocompromised patients warrant longer treatment (5–10 days).
Children under 5 are most at risk of severe complications, necessitating adapted treatment strategies.
items.reference: Shigellosis · ANTIBIOTIC SUSCEPTIBILITY OF SHIGELLA | ANTIBIOTIC TREATMENT OF SHIGELLOSIS (TABLE 172-1)

items.subtopic: REHYDRATION AND NUTRITION | NONSPECIFIC, SYMPTOM-BASED THERAPY | TREATMENT OF COMPLICATIONS
items.title: Oral Rehydration Therapy in Shigellosis
items.content: In shigellosis, oral rehydration is preferred unless the patient is comatose or in shock. The WHO and UNICEF recommend a reduced-osmolarity solution with specific concentrations for effective rehydration.
keyPoints: Oral rehydration is recommended over intravenous unless severe (coma/shock).
WHO/UNICEF standard oral rehydration solution: 245 mOsm/L, sodium 75 mmol/L, chloride 65 mmol/L, glucose 75 mmol/L, potassium 20 mmol/L, citrate 10 mmol/L.
Early refeeding is safe and beneficial; breastfeeding should be maintained unless contraindicated.
items.reference: Shigellosis · REHYDRATION AND NUTRITION | NONSPECIFIC, SYMPTOM-BASED THERAPY | TREATMENT OF COMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: REHYDRATION AND NUTRITION | NONSPECIFIC, SYMPTOM-BASED THERAPY | TREATMENT OF COMPLICATIONS
items.title: Management of Toxic Megacolon in Shigellosis
items.content: Toxic megacolon lacks a consensus treatment. Frequent assessments by medical and surgical teams are crucial, with an emphasis on treating anemia, dehydration, and electrolyte deficits.
keyPoints: No consensus for toxic megacolon; frequent assessment needed.
Treat anemia, dehydration, hypokalemia to prevent colonic atony aggravation.
Nasogastric aspiration can help deflate the colon.
Colectomy recommended if distention persists after 48-72 hours without improvement.
Some physicians may continue medical therapy for up to 7 days if clinical improvement is seen.
items.reference: Shigellosis · REHYDRATION AND NUTRITION | NONSPECIFIC, SYMPTOM-BASED THERAPY | TREATMENT OF COMPLICATIONS

items.title: Hand Hygiene and Stool Decontamination in Shigella Outbreaks
items.content: Effective prevention strategies for limiting the spread of Shigella include rigorous hand washing after defecation or handling feces, especially children's, and before food preparation. Additionally, stool decontamination using agents like sodium hypochlorite is crucial during outbreaks.
keyPoints: Hand washing recommended post-defecation and pre-food handling to prevent spread.
Stool decontamination with sodium hypochlorite limits infection spread in outbreaks.
Negative stool culture before considering cure, as recurrences are rare with proper measures.

items.title: Vaccine Development and Protection Strategies for Shigellosis
items.content: Currently, no vaccine is available against shigellosis despite ongoing trials of live attenuated oral and subunit parenteral candidates. Synthetic polysaccharide conjugate vaccines show promise by eliciting protective IgG antibodies, crucial for children under 3 years who are most at risk.
keyPoints: No current vaccine; several candidates in clinical trials.
Synthetic polysaccharide conjugate vaccines promising due to high levels of protective IgG antibodies.
Protection critical for children <3 years, the group most susceptible to severe disease.

items.question: Which feature distinguishes Shigella from Escherichia coli based on biochemical characteristics?
options: Shigella is nonmotile and does not produce gas from sugars.
Shigella can be distinguished by genome comparison.
Shigella produces indole in all serovars.
items.explanation: Shigella is nonmotile and does not produce gas from sugars, unlike E. coli.

items.question: Which Shigella species produces Shiga toxin?
items.explanation: Shigella dysenteriae type 1 is the species that produces Shiga toxin.

items.question: What is one of the major genomic signatures of Shigella?
items.explanation: A major genomic signature of Shigella is the presence of a 215-kb virulence plasmid.

items.question: What is the primary mode of transmission for Shigella?
items.explanation: Shigella is primarily transmitted by the fecal-oral route, especially via hand carriage.

items.question: Which Shigella species predominates in the least developed areas?
items.explanation: S. flexneri isolates predominate in the least developed areas.

items.question: Which age group has the highest prevalence of Shigella as a pathogen for moderate to severe diarrhea?
items.explanation: Shigella is ranked first among children aged 12–59 months for moderate to severe diarrhea.

items.question: What factor has contributed significantly to the decline in cyclical epidemics of Shigellosis?
items.explanation: The decline in severe cyclic disease is attributed to a significant global reduction of extreme poverty.

items.question: Which mechanism allows Shigella to survive passage through the gastric barrier?
items.explanation: Shigella survives passage through the gastric barrier due to its resistance to low-pH conditions.

items.question: What is the primary role of the type III secretion system in Shigella pathogenesis?
options: To produce toxins that inhibit protein synthesis
To allow bacterial effectors to enter host cell cytoplasm
To induce apoptosis in intestinal epithelial cells
items.explanation: The type III secretion system allows Shigella effectors to transit from the bacterial cytoplasm into the host cell cytoplasm.

items.question: How does Shiga toxin contribute to disease severity in S. dysenteriae type 1 infections?
options: By binding to receptors on epithelial cells and inhibiting protein synthesis
By inducing apoptosis of intestinal epithelial cells
By facilitating the uptake of bacteria into host cells
items.explanation: Shiga toxin binds to globotriaosylceramide on target cell surfaces and inhibits protein synthesis, increasing disease severity.

items.question: Which phase of shigellosis is characterized by bloody mucopurulent stools and increased tenesmus?
items.explanation: The dysentery phase is characterized by bloody mucopurulent stools and increased tenesmus.

items.question: What is the typical incubation period for shigellosis?
items.explanation: The incubation period usually lasts 1–4 days but may be as long as 8 days.

items.question: Which of the following is a rare complication of shigellosis that involves severe inflammation and dilation of the colon?
items.explanation: Toxic megacolon is a rare complication involving severe inflammation and dilation of the colon.

items.question: In which population is bacteremia due to shigellosis most frequently reported?
items.explanation: Bacteremia is most frequently reported in severely malnourished and HIV-infected patients.

items.question: Which histocompatibility antigen is associated with reactive arthritis following shigellosis?
items.explanation: Reactive arthritis can develop in patients expressing the HLA-B27 antigen after shigellosis.

items.question: Postinfectious arthritis associated with shigellosis occurs only after infection with which Shigella serotype?
items.explanation: Postinfectious arthritis occurs only after infection with S. flexneri.

items.question: What is the gold standard for diagnosing Shigella infection?
options: Isolation and identification from fecal material
items.explanation: The isolation and identification of the pathogen from fecal material is the gold standard for diagnosing Shigella infection.

items.question: Which medium should be used to transport stool samples when laboratory facilities are not immediately available?
items.explanation: Buffered glycerol saline or Cary-Blair medium can be used as a holding medium for stool samples.

items.subtopic: ANTIBIOTIC SUSCEPTIBILITY OF SHIGELLA | ANTIBIOTIC TREATMENT OF SHIGELLOSIS (TABLE 172-1)
items.question: Which factor has contributed to the increasing resistance of Shigella to multiple drugs since the mid-1960s?
options: Horizontal transfer of resistance determinants via plasmids and transposons
items.explanation: The passage mentions that horizontal transfer of resistance determinants, particularly via plasmids and transposons, contributes to multidrug resistance.

options: Resistance to early-generation quinolones due to mutations affecting DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV
No significant change in antibiotic resistance patterns
items.explanation: The passage states that chromosomal mutations affecting DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV have led to resistance to early-generation quinolones.

items.question: Which antibiotic is recommended as first-line treatment for shigellosis in the United States?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that fluoroquinolones, preferably ciprofloxacin, are recommended as first-line treatment for shigellosis in the United States.

items.subtopic: REHYDRATION AND NUTRITION | NONSPECIFIC, SYMPTOM-BASED THERAPY | TREATMENT OF COMPLICATIONS
items.question: Which of the following is a recommended component concentration in WHO and UNICEF's standard oral rehydration solution for shigellosis?
options: Sodium 75 mmol/L, Chloride 65 mmol/L, Glucose 75 mmol/L, Potassium 20 mmol/L
Sodium 90 mmol/L, Chloride 80 mmol/L, Glucose 60 mmol/L, Potassium 30 mmol/L
Sodium 50 mmol/L, Chloride 40 mmol/L, Glucose 100 mmol/L, Potassium 10 mmol/L
Sodium 100 mmol/L, Chloride 70 mmol/L, Glucose 80 mmol/L, Potassium 25 mmol/L
items.explanation: The recommended concentrations are sodium 75 mmol/L, chloride 65 mmol/L, glucose 75 mmol/L, and potassium 20 mmol/L.
items.reference: Shigellosis · REHYDRATION AND NUTRITION | NONSPECIFIC, SYMPTOM-BASED THERAPY | TREATMENT OF COMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: REHYDRATION AND NUTRITION | NONSPECIFIC, SYMPTOM-BASED THERAPY | TREATMENT OF COMPLICATIONS
items.question: Which of the following is a reason to avoid antimotility agents in patients with bloody diarrhea due to shigellosis?
options: They can cause prolonged fever and increase the risk of toxic megacolon.
They are ineffective in managing dehydration associated with shigellosis.
They interfere with oral rehydration therapy by affecting sodium-glucose transport.
They lead to rapid resolution of symptoms, which may mask underlying complications.
items.explanation: Antimotility agents can cause prolonged fever and increase the risk of toxic megacolon in patients with bloody diarrhea due to shigellosis.
items.reference: Shigellosis · REHYDRATION AND NUTRITION | NONSPECIFIC, SYMPTOM-BASED THERAPY | TREATMENT OF COMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: REHYDRATION AND NUTRITION | NONSPECIFIC, SYMPTOM-BASED THERAPY | TREATMENT OF COMPLICATIONS
items.question: What is the recommended management for rectal prolapse in shigellosis?
options: Immediate surgical intervention to prevent recurrence.
Use of a warm solution of saturated magnesium sulfate to reduce edema before manual reduction.
Administration of oral rehydration solutions to maintain hydration during treatment.
Application of topical antibiotics to the rectal mucosa.
items.explanation: Edema of the rectal mucosa can be osmotically reduced by application of gauze impregnated with a warm solution of saturated magnesium sulfate before manual reduction.
items.reference: Shigellosis · REHYDRATION AND NUTRITION | NONSPECIFIC, SYMPTOM-BASED THERAPY | TREATMENT OF COMPLICATIONS

items.question: Which measure is recommended to limit the spread of Shigella during outbreaks?
options: Stool decontamination with sodium hypochlorite and a cleaning protocol for medical staff and patients.
Routine vaccination of all individuals in endemic areas.
Use of broad-spectrum antibiotics as a preventive measure.
Isolation of infected individuals without any additional measures.
items.explanation: Stool decontamination with sodium hypochlorite and a cleaning protocol for medical staff and patients have proven useful in limiting the spread during outbreaks.

items.question: What is urgently needed due to rapid progression of antibiotic resistance in Shigella?
items.explanation: Given the rapid progression of antibiotic resistance, a vaccine is urgently needed to combat Shigella infections.

items.question: Which population would benefit most from protection against shigellosis?
items.explanation: Protection is particularly important for children <3 years, who are most susceptible to severe forms of shigellosis.

items.statement: Shigella and E. coli can be distinguished by genome comparison.
items.explanation: Shigella cannot be distinguished from E. coli by genome comparison.

items.statement: Shigella flexneri and Shigella sonnei share ~93% of their genes.
items.explanation: Genome sequencing reveals that these species have ~93% of genes in common.
The passage confirms that shigellosis can indeed be transmitted through sexual contact.

items.statement: S. sonnei is more prevalent in the least developed areas.
items.explanation: S. flexneri, not S. sonnei, predominates in the least developed areas.

items.statement: Shigella can survive well in the external environment.
items.explanation: Shigella is poorly adapted to survive in the environment.

items.statement: Intracellular shigellae use cytoskeletal components for cell-to-cell spread.
items.explanation: Shigella uses cytoskeletal components to propel themselves and facilitate cell-to-cell spread.

items.statement: Dehydration is a major feature of dysenteric syndromes caused by shigellosis.
items.explanation: Unlike most diarrheal syndromes, dysenteric syndromes rarely present with dehydration as a major feature.

items.statement: HUS is more commonly associated with S. dysenteriae type 1 in industrialized countries.
items.explanation: Shiga toxin produced by S. dysenteriae type 1 has been linked to HUS in developing countries but rarely in industrialized countries.

items.statement: Blood cultures are positive in more than 5% of cases when diagnosing Shigella infection.
items.explanation: Blood cultures are positive in fewer than 5% of cases.

items.statement: Shigella colonies on nonselective or low-selectivity medium appear as lactose-fermenting colonies.
items.explanation: Shigella colonies are non-lactose-fermenting.

items.subtopic: ANTIBIOTIC SUSCEPTIBILITY OF SHIGELLA | ANTIBIOTIC TREATMENT OF SHIGELLOSIS (TABLE 172-1)
items.statement: Resistance to second-generation quinolones such as ciprofloxacin has been detected in less than 5% of Shigella isolates in India and Bangladesh.
items.explanation: The passage states that resistance to norfloxacin, ciprofloxacin, and ofloxacin was found in 5% of isolates during subsequent outbreaks in India and Bangladesh.

items.statement: Parenteral nutrition is proven to be beneficial in the treatment of toxic megacolon.
items.explanation: Parenteral nutrition has not been proven to be beneficial in treating toxic megacolon.

items.statement: Breast-feeding should be discontinued in infants with shigellosis.
items.explanation: Breast-feeding reduces diarrheal losses and the need for oral rehydration, so it should be maintained unless contraindicated.

items.statement: A vaccine against shigellosis is currently available.
items.explanation: No vaccine against shigellosis is currently available, although several candidates are undergoing clinical trials.

items.statement: Recurrences of Shigella infection are common if therapeutic and preventive measures are correctly implemented.
items.explanation: Recurrences are rare if therapeutic and preventive measures are correctly implemented.

items.assertion: Shigellosis is primarily an endemic disease.
items.reason: 99% of cases occur in developing countries with poor hygiene standards.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why shigellosis remains endemic.

items.assertion: The incidence of Shigella is highest among young adults.
items.reason: Young adults have lower immunity to Shigella compared to children and elderly.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the incidence peaks in children and increases again in the elderly, not young adults.

items.assertion: Toxic megacolon can be a complication of shigellosis.
items.reason: It is caused by severe inflammation extending to the colonic smooth-muscle layer, leading to paralysis and dilation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: HUS is a nonimmune hemolytic anemia associated with shigellosis.
items.reason: It is characterized by microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and acute renal failure.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Reactive arthritis can develop weeks or months after shigellosis.
items.reason: This is especially true in patients expressing the HLA-B27 antigen.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Serotyping may soon be replaced by genome-based techniques.
items.reason: Molecular methods of diagnostics are already standardized for global use.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as molecular methods are not yet standardized.

items.reason: Fluoroquinolones are effective against multidrug-resistant strains of Shigella.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason given does not directly explain why fluoroquinolones are recommended as first-line treatment; it is more about their effectiveness and resistance patterns.

items.reason: Water restriction includes discontinuation of oral rehydration solutions and potassium-rich nutrition in HUS treatment.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why water restriction is necessary in treating HUS.
